questions.question: What is the most general frequency-domain expression for the Wiener filter for stationary processes?

explanations.a: Incorrect. This ratio does not represent the Wiener solution.
explanations.b: Incorrect. This is a special case valid when the desired signal and noise are uncorrelated and additive.
explanations.c: Correct! This is the most general Wiener filter form using cross-power spectral density.
explanations.d: Incorrect. This relates to spectral estimation, not Wiener filtering.

questions.question: In FIR Wiener filtering, how is the optimal filter coefficient vector H_opt computed from the input and desired signals?

explanations.a: Incorrect. Simply dividing the vectors does not yield the Wiener solution.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Multiplying the autocorrelation matrix by the cross-correlation vector is not correct; inversion is needed.
explanations.c: Correct! The optimal coefficient vector is given by H_opt = R^{-1} P, where R is the input autocorrelation matrix and P is the cross-correlation vector.
explanations.d: Incorrect. The cross-correlation vector is not inverted; only R is inverted.

questions.question: The Wiener-Hopf equation arises from which of the following optimization principles?

answers.d: Minimizing filter energy subject to distortion constraints.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Noise estimation is not the primary criterion.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Wiener filtering minimizes MSE, not maximizes SNR directly.
explanations.c: Correct! The orthogonality principle gives rise to the Wiener-Hopf equations.
explanations.d: Incorrect. This is related to constrained optimization, not Wiener filtering.

questions.question: In practice, what issue arises when estimating the Wiener filter coefficients using sample data?

answers.a: High computational complexity in estimating Fourier transforms.
answers.b: Noise whitening can distort the desired signal.
answers.c: Sample autocorrelation matrix may be ill-conditioned or non-invertible.
answers.d: The Wiener filter only works on infinite-length signals.

explanations.a: Incorrect. DFT is not the core issue in coefficient estimation.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Noise whitening is not a requirement in Wiener filtering.
explanations.c: Correct! The sample correlation matrix can be nearly singular, affecting stability.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Finite-length implementations are possible but need caution.

questions.question: What does the autocorrelation function φ_xx[k] represent in Wiener filtering?

answers.a: The power spectral density of the desired signal
answers.b: The similarity between the signal and its shifted version
answers.c: The error between desired and estimated signal
answers.d: The frequency response of the filter

explanations.a: Incorrect. PSD is the Fourier transform of autocorrelation.
explanations.b: Correct! Autocorrelation measures similarity between a signal and its shifted version.
explanations.d: Incorrect. This refers to filter characteristics, not correlation.

questions.question: What is the role of the cross-correlation function φ_xd[k] in Wiener filtering?

answers.a: It measures similarity between noise and signal
answers.b: It relates input signal to desired signal at different lags

explanations.a: Incorrect. It relates input and desired signal, not specifically noise.
explanations.b: Correct! Cross-correlation measures relation between input and desired signal at lag k.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Filter length is chosen separately.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Noise power comes from autocorrelation/PSD.

questions.question: In the FIR Wiener filter formulation, what does the matrix R represent?

answers.a: Cross-correlation matrix between input and desired signal
answers.b: Autocorrelation matrix of the input signal

explanations.a: Incorrect. That corresponds to vector P.
explanations.b: Correct! R = E{X[n] X^T[n]} is the autocorrelation matrix of the input.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Noise covariance is not explicitly used here.
explanations.d: Incorrect. R is data-dependent, not identity.

questions.question: In the additive noise model x[n] = s[n] + v[n], what assumption allows the Wiener filter to simplify to H(z) = Φ_ss(z) / (Φ_ss(z) + Φ_vv(z))?

answers.a: The signal and noise are correlated
answers.b: The signal and noise are uncorrelated

explanations.a: Incorrect. Correlation would introduce cross terms in the spectrum.
explanations.b: Correct! Uncorrelated signal and noise ensure Φ_xd(z) = Φ_ss(z), leading to this form.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Wiener filtering assumes random processes, not deterministic signals.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Noise need not be white; only uncorrelated with the signal.

questions.question: What does the term P^T R^{-1} P represent in the minimum MSE expression?

answers.a: Total noise power in the system
answers.b: The portion of desired signal power recoverable from input

explanations.a: Incorrect. Noise power is not directly represented here.
explanations.b: Correct! It represents the recoverable part of desired signal power.
explanations.c: Incorrect. This term reduces the error, not equals it.
explanations.d: Incorrect. It is not related to filter energy.

questions.question: In Wiener filtering, under what condition does the optimal filter become a simple gain factor (i.e., scalar multiplication)?

answers.a: When the noise is white and the signal is deterministic.
answers.b: When both signal and noise are uncorrelated white processes.
answers.c: When the autocorrelation matrix becomes diagonal.
answers.d: When the signal and noise have equal power spectra.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Deterministic signals violate the random process assumption.
explanations.b: Incorrect. White and uncorrelated processes lead to diagonal correlation matrices but not necessarily a scalar gain.
explanations.c: Correct! A diagonal autocorrelation matrix results in scalar gain per frequency.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Equal power does not lead to scalar filtering; it leads to SNR = 1.
